questionText: Henry started a trip into the country between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M. when the hands of the clock were together. He arrived at his destination between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M. when the hands of the clock were exactly 180° apart. How long did he travel?

steps.explanation: Find the time hands coincide between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M. The minute hand must gain 40 minute-spaces over the hour hand (since at 8:00, minute hand is 40 spaces behind).
steps.result: Henry started at $43\frac{7}{11}$ min past 8 A.M.

steps.explanation: Find the time hands are 180° apart between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M. At 2:00 P.M. the minute hand is 10 minute-spaces behind the hour hand. To be 30 spaces apart (180°) the minute hand must gain (10 + 30) = 40 spaces.
steps.calculation: Minute hand gains 40 spaces in $43\frac{7}{11}$ min past 2 P.M.
steps.result: Henry arrived at $43\frac{7}{11}$ min past 2 P.M.

steps.explanation: Calculate the duration of travel from $43\frac{7}{11}$ min past 8 A.M. to $43\frac{7}{11}$ min past 2 P.M.
steps.calculation: 2 P.M. - 8 A.M. = 6 hours (the fractional minutes cancel)

solution.shortcut: Since both departure and arrival times have the same fractional minutes offset, the travel time is exactly 2 P.M. − 8 A.M. = 6 hours.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that at 2:00 the hands are 10 spaces apart (not 0), so the minute hand needs to gain 40 spaces (not 30) to be 180° apart.

questionText: Imagine that your watch was correct at noon, but then it began to lose 30 minutes each hour. It now shows 4 p.m. but it stopped 5 hours ago. What is the correct time now?

steps.explanation: The watch loses 30 minutes every hour, meaning it advances only 30 minutes for each real hour. So for every 2 real hours, the watch shows 1 hour.
steps.formulaExpression: \text{Watch time gained per real hour} = 30 \text{ min}
steps.calculation: To show 4 hours (from noon to 4 p.m.), the watch needed 4 ÷ 0.5 = 8 real hours.
steps.result: The watch stopped at 8 real hours after noon = 8 p.m.

steps.explanation: The watch stopped 5 hours ago (in real time), so the current real time is 5 hours after 8 p.m.

solution.shortcut: Watch loses half the real time, so showing 4-hour advance means 8 real hours elapsed. Add 5 more stopped hours: 8 p.m. + 5 h = 1 a.m.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'loses 30 min per hour' with the watch running backwards; it still advances, just at half the real rate.

questionText: A mechanical grandfather clock is at present showing 7 hr 40 min 6 sec. Assuming that it loses 4 seconds in every hour, what time will it show after exactly $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours?

steps.explanation: Calculate total time lost by the clock in $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours at 4 seconds per hour.
Find the correct time after $6\frac{1}{2}$ hours by adding to the current time.
steps.calculation: 7 hr 40 min 6 sec + 6 hr 30 min = 14 hr 10 min 6 sec

steps.explanation: Subtract the 26 seconds lost to get the time shown by the clock.
steps.calculation: 14 hr 10 min 6 sec − 26 sec = 14 hr 9 min 40 sec

solution.shortcut: Add the real elapsed time to the current reading, then subtract the cumulative loss (4 sec/hr × 6.5 hr = 26 sec).
solution.commonMistake: Adding the time lost instead of subtracting it, or forgetting to borrow correctly when subtracting seconds from minutes.

modelName: Clock loses — show time after interval

questionText: I have two watches with a 12-hour cycle. One of them gains one minute a day and the other loses $1\frac{1}{2}$ minutes per day. If I set them both at the correct time, how long will it be before they again tell the correct time together?

steps.explanation: A watch with a 12-hour cycle shows correct time when it has gained or lost exactly 12 hours = 720 minutes.
steps.result: Each watch must gain/lose 720 minutes to show correct time

steps.explanation: Watch 1 gains 1 min/day, so it shows correct time every 720 days.
Watch 2 loses $1\frac{1}{2}$ min/day = $\frac{3}{2}$ min/day, so it shows correct time every $720 \div \frac{3}{2} = 480$ days.
Both show correct time together after LCM(720, 480) days.

solution.shortcut: Find how many days each watch takes to gain/lose 720 min, then take LCM.
solution.commonMistake: Using 24 hours (1440 min) instead of 12 hours (720 min) as the cycle for showing correct time.

questionText: A watch is 1 minute slow at 1 p.m. on Tuesday and 2 minutes fast at 1 p.m. on Thursday. When did it show the correct time?

steps.explanation: Time from 1 p.m. Tuesday to 1 p.m. Thursday = 48 hours. During this period the watch changes from −1 min to +2 min, gaining a total of 3 minutes in 48 hours.
Find the time to gain 1 minute (to go from −1 min to 0).
Add 16 hours to 1 p.m. Tuesday.
steps.result: Correct time was shown at 5 a.m. on Wednesday

solution.shortcut: Rate = 3 min/48 hr; needs 1 min gain from base → 48/3 = 16 hr after 1 p.m. Tuesday = 5 a.m. Wednesday.
solution.commonMistake: Not recognising that the watch was slow (−1 min) at the start, so it needs to gain 1 minute (not 2) to reach zero.

modelName: Watch gain rate — find correct time

questionText: A watch which gains uniformly is 2 minutes low at noon on Monday and is 4 min 48 sec fast at 2 p.m. on the following Monday. When was it correct?

steps.explanation: Time from noon Monday to 2 p.m. the following Monday = 7 days 2 hours = 170 hours.
Total gain = 2 min (to compensate the initial slow) + 4 min 48 sec (fast at end) = $6\frac{4}{5}$ min = $\frac{34}{5}$ min.
Find the time to gain 2 minutes (to go from −2 min to 0).
Add 50 hours to noon Monday.
steps.calculation: Noon Monday + 48 h = noon Wednesday; + 2 more h = 2 p.m. Wednesday
steps.result: Watch was correct at 2 p.m. on Wednesday

solution.shortcut: Total gain over 170 h = 34/5 min; need 2 min gain → 170/(34/5) × 2 = 50 h after noon Monday = 2 p.m. Wednesday.
solution.commonMistake: Converting 4 min 48 sec incorrectly; 48 sec = 4/5 min, so total = 4 + 4/5 = 24/5 min fast, plus 2 min slow = 34/5 min total.

questionText: A watch which gains 5 seconds in 3 minutes was set right at 7 a.m. In the afternoon of the same day, when the watch indicated quarter past 4 o'clock, the true time is

steps.explanation: Time shown on watch from 7 a.m. to 4:15 p.m. = 9 hours 15 minutes = $\frac{37}{4}$ hours.
The watch gains 5 sec every 3 min, so in 3 min 5 sec of watch time, only 3 min of real time passes. That means $\frac{37}{720}$ hours of watch time = $\frac{1}{20}$ hour of real time.
Add 9 real hours to 7 a.m.

solution.shortcut: Ratio of real to watch time = 180/185 = 36/37. Real elapsed = (37/4) × (36/37) = 9 h. So 7 a.m. + 9 h = 4 p.m.
solution.commonMistake: Using the gain rate inversely; remember watch runs faster, so real time < watch time.

questionText: Between 5 and 6, a lady looked at her watch and mistaking the hour hand for the minute hand, she thought that the time was 57 minutes earlier than the correct time. The correct time was

steps.explanation: If the lady misread the time as 57 minutes earlier than correct, the minute hand she was reading (actually the hour hand) was showing a time 57 minutes behind the actual minute hand position. So the minute hand is $(60 - 57) = 3$ minute-spaces behind the hour hand.
steps.result: Minute hand is 3 spaces behind hour hand

steps.explanation: At 5 o'clock, the minute hand is 25 spaces behind the hour hand. For the minute hand to be only 3 spaces behind, it must gain $25 - 3 = 22$ spaces.
55 minute-spaces are gained in 60 minutes.

solution.shortcut: The hour hand position (misread as minute) is 57 min earlier visually → real minute hand leads by 3 spaces. Gain needed from 5 o'clock: 25−3=22 spaces → 24 min.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which hand is leading; the minute hand must be 3 spaces BEHIND the hour hand (not ahead).

modelName: Misread clock hands — find correct time

questionText: How much does a watch lose per day if its hands coincide every 64 minutes?

steps.explanation: In a correct clock, the hands coincide every $\frac{720}{11}$ minutes $\approx 65\frac{5}{11}$ minutes (since the minute hand gains 55 spaces in 60 min, it takes $\frac{60}{55} \times 60 = 65\frac{5}{11}$ min to gain 60 spaces).
steps.result: Correct clock: hands coincide every $65\frac{5}{11}$ min

steps.explanation: This watch's hands coincide every 64 min (less than $65\frac{5}{11}$), so the watch is losing time.
steps.result: Loss = $\frac{16}{11}$ min per 64 min of watch time

steps.explanation: Calculate loss per 24 hours (1440 minutes of real time).

solution.shortcut: Correct interval = 720/11 min. Loss per interval = 720/11 − 64 = 16/11 min. Loss per day = (16/11) × (24×60/64) = 360/11 = 32 8/11 min.
solution.commonMistake: Using 60 min as the correct coincidence interval instead of 720/11 ≈ 65 5/11 min.

questionText: A wall clock gains 2 minutes in 12 hours, while a table clock loses 2 minutes every 36 hours. Both are set right at 12 noon on Tuesday. The correct time when both show the same time next would be

steps.explanation: Wall clock gains 2 min per 12 h = 4 min per 24 h. Table clock loses 2 min per 36 h = $\frac{4}{3}$ min per 24 h.
The two clocks show the same time when their total difference is a multiple of 12 hours = 720 min (since both are 12-hour clocks).
Both were set at 12 noon, so after 135 days both show the same time = 12 noon (since wall gained 720 min = 12 h, and table lost 180 min, total difference 720 min).
steps.calculation: Wall shows: 12 noon + 135×4 min = 12 noon + 540 min = 9 a.m. (next day reads noon). Table shows: 12 noon − 135×(4/3) min = 12 noon − 180 min = 9 a.m. Both are 12 noon equivalent on 12-hr cycle.

solution.shortcut: Combined gain/loss rate = 16/3 min/day; need 720 min difference → 135 days. Both clocks read noon.
solution.commonMistake: Using 24 hours instead of 12 hours as the cycle for clocks to re-align.

modelName: Two clocks gain/lose — same time again

questionText: The angle between the hands of a clock when the time is 4:25 am is

steps.explanation: Use the formula for the angle between clock hands: $\theta = \left|30H - \frac{11M}{2}\right|$ where H = hours, M = minutes.

solution.commonMistake: Using $30H - 6M$ (which gives hour-hand position minus minute-hand position incorrectly) instead of the standard formula.

questionText: At 8:30, the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock form an angle of

steps.explanation: At 8:30, use the angle formula $\theta = \left|30H - \frac{11M}{2}\right|$.

solution.shortcut: Hour hand at 8:30 is at $8 \times 30 + 30 \times 0.5 = 255°$; minute hand at $30 \times 6 = 180°$; difference = 75°.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the hour hand also moves during the 30 minutes (it is not at exactly 240° but at 255°).

questionText: A clock is displaying correct time at 9 am on Monday. If the clock loses 12 minutes in 24 hours, then the actual time when the clock indicates 8:30 pm on Wednesday of the same week is

steps.explanation: Time shown by clock from 9 am Monday to 8:30 pm Wednesday = 59 hours 30 min = $\frac{119}{2}$ hours.
The clock loses 12 min per 24 h, so it shows 23 h 48 min = $\frac{119}{5}$ hours for every 24 real hours.
Convert clock hours to real hours.
Add 60 real hours to 9 am Monday.
steps.calculation: 9 am Monday + 60 h = 9 am Monday + 2.5 days = 9 pm Wednesday
steps.result: Actual time is 9 pm on Wednesday

solution.shortcut: Clock shows 119/5 h per 24 real h. For 119/2 clock hours: real = (119/2)×(24×5/119) = 60 h → 9 am Mon + 60 h = 9 pm Wed.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to set up the clock-to-real-time ratio correctly; 24 h clock = 23 h 48 min real when clock is slow.

modelName: Losing clock — find real time from clock reading

questionText: There are two clocks, both set to show 10 pm on 21st January 2010. One clock gains 2 minutes in an hour and the other clock loses 5 minutes in an hour. Then by how many minutes do the two clocks differ at 4 pm on 22nd January 2010?

steps.explanation: Find the real time elapsed from 10 pm on 21st January to 4 pm on 22nd January.
steps.calculation: 10 pm to 4 pm next day = 18 hours

steps.explanation: Clock A gains 2 min/hour, so in 18 hours it gains $2 \times 18 = 36$ min. Clock B loses 5 min/hour, so in 18 hours it loses $5 \times 18 = 90$ min.
steps.calculation: Gain A = 36 min; Loss B = 90 min
steps.result: A shows 36 min ahead; B shows 90 min behind

steps.explanation: Total difference between the two clocks = gain + loss = $36 + 90 = 126$ minutes.

solution.shortcut: Combined rate = 2 + 5 = 7 min/hour divergence. Over 18 hours: 7 × 18 = 126 min.
solution.commonMistake: Computing only the gain or only the loss instead of adding both (since one is ahead and the other behind).

modelName: Two clocks gain/lose — difference after time
